coredata.dc:title: Enhanced afterglow performance of carbon dots via surface modification engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yulong
Li, Chao
Zang, Jinhao
Zheng, Guangsong
Nan, Yang
Xia, Xiaofan
Zhu, Zhichao
Cao, Guoqin
Hu, Junhua
Zhu, Jinyang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit certain limitations in terms of afterglow emission intensity and water stability. Here, we present a strategy to enhance and stabilize the afterglow emission of CDs by sequentially modifying seed CDs with urea and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane. Compared to urea-modified CDs, the afterglow intensity of uCDs@SiO2 was significantly enhanced by 25-fold, with the afterglow brightness increasing from 5.51 to 50.93 cd/m2 under 5-W illumination. More importantly, after calcination at 200 °C, uCDs@SiO2 retain approximately 76 % of their initial afterglow emission intensity in water solution over an 8-h period. This retention is attributed to a dense SiO2 matrix that mitigates oxygen-related surface defects and restricts non-radiative decay pathways. Finally, leveraging this robust afterglow, we demonstrate time-delay light-emitting diodes that continue to glow after power-off, as well as an information-encryption method based on selective quenching.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Afterglow
Water stability
Covalent bonds
Calcination

This work was also supported by Frontier Exploration Project of Longmen Laboratory ( LMOYTSKT009 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Images of uCDs and uCDs@SiO2 powder, taken before and after excitation using a 365 nm UV lamp. (b) Afterglow emission spectra of uCDs (blue) and uCDs@SiO2 (red) under ambient conditions, excited with a 365 nm UV lamp. (c) Afterglow decays spectra of uCDs and uCDs@SiO2. (d) CIE chromaticity diagram showing the afterglow coordinates of uCDs and uCDs@SiO2.

a–c) TEM (a) HR-TEM (b) images, and size distribution (c) of uCDs. d–f) TEM (d), HR-TEM (e) images, and size distribution (f) of uCDs@SiO2. g–i) XRD patterns (g), FT-IR (h), and XPS survey (i) spectra of uCDs and uCDs@SiO2.

Theoretical calculation for efficient yellow afterglow behaviors of uCDs and uCDs@SiO2. (a) Structural models and SOC value (ξ) between singlet and triplet states of uCDs and uCDs@SiO2. (b) The afterglow emission mechanism diagram of uCDs and uCDs@SiO2.

(a) Images of uCDs@SiO2 powder, taken before and after excitation using a 365 nm UV lamp. (b) Afterglow emission spectrum of uCDs@SiO2 in water solution at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. (c) Afterglow decay spectra of uCDs@SiO2 in water solution at λEx = 365 nm, λEm = 580 nm. (d) Afterglow emission stability of uCDs@SiO2 in water solution.
(a) Photographs of afterglow emission from the integrated LED devices after power-up and outage of electricity. (b) uCDs@SiO2 for message encryption.

head.ce:title: Enhanced afterglow performance of carbon dots via surface modification engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Centre for International Cooperation on Designer Low-Carbon & Environmental Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450052, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Centre for International Cooperation on Designer Low-Carbon & Environmental Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Centre for International Cooperation on Designer Low-Carbon & Environmental Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450052, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Materials and Devices, Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Materials and Devices, Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit certain limitations in terms of afterglow emission intensity and water stability. Here, we present a strategy to enhance and stabilize the afterglow emission of CDs by sequentially modifying seed CDs with urea and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane. Compared to urea-modified CDs, the afterglow intensity of uCDs@SiO2 was significantly enhanced by 25-fold, with the afterglow brightness increasing from 5.51 to 50.93 cd/m2 under 5-W illumination. More importantly, after calcination at 200 °C, uCDs@SiO2 retain approximately 76 % of their initial afterglow emission intensity in water solution over an 8-h period. This retention is attributed to a dense SiO2 matrix that mitigates oxygen-related surface defects and restricts non-radiative decay pathways. Finally, leveraging this robust afterglow, we demonstrate time-delay light-emitting diodes that continue to glow after power-off, as well as an information-encryption method based on selective quenching.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow materials have attracted a great deal of attention from researchers due to their excellent luminescent properties, which are widely used in applications such as sensing, bio-imaging and light-emitting devices [1–3]. Traditional afterglow materials are typically developed by designing inorganic complexes composed of transition metals and rare earth ions [4–6]. However, their further development is hindered by the limited availability of resources and the high toxicity associated with precious metals. Additionally, the synthesis of metal-based afterglow materials is both complex and costly, driving an urgent need for the development of environmentally friendly and cost-effective alternatives. As a result, non-metallic organic phosphorescent materials have increasingly captured the attention of the research community.

Carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon nanoparticles or carbon quantum dots, are a class of nanomaterials characterized by their unique optical, electronic, and chemical properties. As emerging carbon nano-luminescent materials, CDs stand out from other luminescent materials due to their unique optical performance, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and ease of preparation [7,8]. In recent years, the development of CDs-based afterglow materials has significantly broadened the potential applications of luminescent materials in practical scenarios [9,10]. High afterglow efficiencies in CDs have achieved by embedding them into various matrices, where the formation of hydrogen bonds, covalent bonds, ionic bonds, or spatial restriction within the CDs systems stabilizes chromophores and suppress non-radiative relaxation of triplet states [11,12]. For instance, Song et al. introduced abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs using a thermal urea bath strategy, which enhancing spin-orbit coupling (SOC), promoted intersystem crossing (ISC) processes, and resulted in a blue phosphorescent efficiency of 50.17 % [13]. Meanwhile, Liu et al. achieved triplet-state exciton emission ranging from the ultraviolet (UV) to the near-infrared (NIR) in CDs by confining multicolored CDs emitters within NaCNO crystals [14]. Liu et al. integrated CDs with ink to develop a three-dimensional dynamic code that incorporates both color and temperature information, enabling advanced dynamic information encryption [15]. Despite these advancements, most CDs exhibit quenching effects in water solution, primarily due to the high concentration of dissolved oxygen, which significantly quenches the excited states responsible for phosphorescence [16,17]. A critical challenge for expanding the applications of these materials lies in achieving effective afterglow emission in water solution.

Here, we propose a systematic strategy to enhance and stabilize the afterglow emission of CDs through sequential modification using urea and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES). Compared with urea-modified CDs, the afterglow intensity of uCDs@SiO2 was significantly enhanced by 25-fold, with the afterglow brightness increasing from 5.51 to 50.93 cd/m2 under 5-W illumination. Moreover, these improved properties were lagged preserved when the CDs were placed in the water environment. We propose that the high-temperature calcination process not only converts the loosely structured CDs into a robust and densely packed silica network, but also diminishes oxygen-related surface defects on the CDs. Consequently, these modifications enhance the stability of the CDs in the aqueous phase. Finally, these CDs can be effectively utilized in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and for information encryption applications.
APTES, citric acid (CA) and urea (99.5 %) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of the reagents were used without further purification.

2 g of urea and 1 g of CA were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol solution, and then the solution was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 160 °C for 8 h, and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Then dry the precipitate to get the seed CDs.

5 mg of seed CDs, 1 g of urea and 10 mL of ethanol solution were stirred for 10 min in a reactor liner, and then the reaction was carried out in an oven at 160 °C for 6 h. At the end of the reaction, the solution was cooled down to room temperature. After that, the obtained dark brown solution purified in a 3500-dialysis bag. And then dried in an oven at 60 °C to give the dark products of uCDs.

100 mg of uCDs was placed in a 10 mL sample bottle, after which 200 μL of APTES solution and 5 mL of water were added and stirred to mix homogeneously, and then the mixed solution was oil-bathed at 150 °C for 2 h. The solid powder obtained was uCDs@SiO2.

The sample was placed in a tube furnace and heated at 200 °C for 2 h under nitrogen atmosphere with a temperature increase rate of 5 °C/min and a holding time of 2 h, and the powder obtained was calcined uCDs@SiO2.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were obtained on a JEOL-2100F microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALab220i-XL electron spectrometer (VG Scientific). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku Ultima IV system. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV-3101PC UV–vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and afterglow emission spectra of CDs were measured on a QE Pro (Ocean Insight); all the afterglow emission spectra were measured at 1 ms delay time after turning the UV lamp off. The PL quantum yield (PL QY) was measured by a FLS5 spectrometer.

Initially, seed CDs were treated with urea to obtain uCDs; subsequently, they were further modified using APTES, resulting in the synthesis of uCDs@SiO2 (Fig. S1). As depicted in Fig. 1 a and Video S1, after the removal of 365 nm radiation, both CDs exhibit yellow afterglow emission. However, it is evident from the figure that afterglow emission duration of APTES-treated CDs is significantly prolonged. Fig. 1b illustrates that the afterglow emission center at approximately 570 nm, where the intensity of uCDs@SiO2 is 25 times greater than that of uCDs, and its lifetime significantly exceeds that of uCDs, which is consistent with the experimental findings. By comparing the brightness of uCDs and uCDs@SiO2, it was found that the brightness of uCDs@SiO2 is about tenfold that of uCDs. Specifically, the brightness values are 5.51 cd/m2 for uCDs (Fig. S2), while uCDs@SiO2 exhibits a brightness of 50.93 cd/m2 (Fig. S3). The photoluminescence quantum yield (PL QY) was subsequently measured, revealing a PL QY of 1.67 % for uCDs (Fig. S4). In contrast, the PL QY of uCDs@SiO2 was significantly enhanced to 18.04 % (Fig. S5). The CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates of afterglow emission of uCDs and uCDs@SiO2 are (0.41, 0.46) and (0.46, 0.48), respectively (Fig. 1d), indicating yellow light emission, which aligns well with the afterglow observed by the naked eye.

Furthermore, we present additional experimental data and analysis on the temperature-dependent afterglow intensity and lifetime of uCDs@SiO2, spanning from 77 to 297 K. As shown in Fig. S6 and Table S1 , with the increase in temperature, both the afterglow intensity and lifetime of CDs exhibit a downward trend, which is highly consistent with the room-temperature phosphorescence characteristics previously reported in the literature [13].

To explore the potential mechanism behind the enhanced afterglow performance of uCDs@SiO2, a comprehensive investigation was carried out to analyze the chemical structure and composition of uCDs@SiO2 before and after APTES treatment. The morphologies of CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The results in Fig. 2 a and c demonstrates the successful encapsulation of uCDs within the urea matrix, exhibiting an average size of 3.5 nm. Furthermore, uCDs@SiO2 was also combined with the SiO2 matrix, and the average size increased to 4.3 nm (Fig. 2 d and f). After the surface modification treatment, the size of CDs was observed to increase significantly [18,19]. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of two CDs show a lattice of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon (Fig. 2b and e) [20]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern exhibits a broad peak at 22°, as shown in Fig. S7a, which is attributed to the graphitic structure with an interlayer spacing (002) of 0.41 nm [21]. The characteristic diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of uCDs are highly consistent with the standard diffraction peak positions of urea [22]. These results indicate a uniform distribution of uCDs within the urea matrix, further confirming the observations made by TEM. Additionally, the XRD spectrum of uCDs@SiO2 reveals a broad diffraction peak corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of SiO2, indicating the presence of a tetrahedral Si-O lattice structure and confirming the successful embedding of CDs into the SiO2 matrix (Fig. 2g) [23].

The structures of CDs were investigated by Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2h) reveals that, both CDs possess a variety of chemical bonds on their surfaces, including O-H, N-H, C=O, and C=N. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectrum of uCDs shows a significant enhancement in absorption peaks at 3348 and 3436 cm−1 compared to seed CDs, as depicted in Fig. S7b [24,25]. These peaks are attributed to the stretching vibrations of amino and carboxyl groups, suggesting a higher concentration of -NH2 and -OH functional groups in the uCDs system, which facilitates the formation of hydrogen bonds [26]. Notably, stretching vibrations corresponding to Si-C and Si-O-C bonds were observed 780 and 1138 cm−1 in uCDs@SiO2, indicating the formation of covalent bonds between the CDs and amorphous SiO2 matrix [18,27].

The XRD and FTIR results are further confirmed by the XPS characterizations (Fig. 2g–j and Fig. S7c). The elemental composition of C, N and O in both the uCDs and uCDs@SiO2 is found to be identical, indicating that the synthesized CDs are abundant in nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups. Furthermore, uCDs has higher ratio of N and O elements (Table S2) compared to seed CDs, further confirming that these CDs are confined in urea with plenty of -OH and -NH2 groups. Additionally, new Si-related peaks at 101.59 eV were detected in uCDs@SiO2, providing further evidence for the formation of covalent bonds involving Si in uCDs@SiO2 [28]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s region for uCDs reveal peaks at 288.9 eV, 286.1 eV, and 284.7 eV, corresponding to C=N/C=O, C-N/C-O, and C-C/C=C bonds, respectively [18,29]. In contrast to the uCDs, a new peak appeared at 284.1 eV is observed in uCDs@SiO2, which can be attributed to the C-Si bond (Fig. S8a) [22]. The O 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum of uCDs displays peaks at 531.5 eV and 531.1 eV, attributed to C-O and C=O bonds [30]. For uCDs@SiO2, the peaks at 532.1 eV and 531.6 eV correspond to Si-O and C-OH bonds, respectively (Fig. S8b) [31,32]. In the Si 2p spectrum, the peaks at 102.6 eV and 101.9 eV are correspond to Si-O and Si-C bonds, respectively (Fig. S8c) [18,33]. These results suggest that the surface of uCDs@SiO2 undergoes the formation of multiple covalent bonds, which plays a critical role in enhancing the afterglow properties.

Building on the preceding analysis, the factors contributing to the observed discrepancies in optical performance between uCDs and uCDs@SiO2 can be further elucidated. These discrepancies may be attributed to the formation of various nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups (such as C=O, C=N, or -NH2) on the surface of uCDs, resulting from the interaction of urea molecules with carbon precursors during the hydrothermal reaction process [34]. The C=O/C=N functional groups not only enhance SOC and promotes ISC, effectively generating triplet excited states endowing the CDs with afterglow properties [35], but also facilitate the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds, which help stabilize the triplet state [25]. However, the stabilization effect of hydrogen bonds is limited due to their relatively weak strength [36]. In contrast, during the reaction process of APTES, the surface of uCDs@SiO2 forms abundant covalent bond, and the CDs are embedded in situ within the SiO2 lattice composed of Si-O tetrahedra. Given that covalent bonds are significantly stronger than hydrogen bonds, they contribute to enhancing the emission intensity, lifetime and PL QY of uCDs@SiO2 [37]. The synergistic effect of urea and APTES significantly enhanced the afterglow emission intensity of CDs.

Additionally, further theoretical simulations were performed using the first principles time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) method to investigate the influence of surface modification engineering on the afterglow emission mechanism of CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the SOC ξ (S1, T1) exhibited a sequential increase, with calculated values of 0.190 and 0.313 cm−1 for uCDs and uCDs@SiO2, respectively. These results were analyzed in accordance with Equation as follows:

)

represents the SOC matrix element between the excited singlet and triplet states,
Δ E
S T
is the energy gap between the S1 and T1 states,
K B
is the Boltzmann constant, and T is temperature [35]. The
k
I S C
can be significantly influenced by the SOC between S1 and Tn, thereby hindering the occurrence of the ISC process for uCDs due to its weak SOC. These theoretical findings are in good agreement with the experimentally observations. In other words, the surface modification process enhances the SOC, thereby increasing the ISC rate and facilitating effective afterglow emission. As shown in Fig. 3b, afterglow is scarcely observed in uCDs due to their inefficient ISC process and the dominance of non-radiative triplet-exciton dissipation. However, through surface modification engineering, the abundant -OH and -NH2 groups on the surface of CDs react with APTES to form a rigid network stabilized by multiple hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds [38].

After immersing uCDs@SiO2 in water solution for a specific duration, the afterglow emission is no longer detectable. Interestingly, when calcined at 200 °C in a tube furnace for 2 h, uCDs@SiO2 exhibits remarkable stability even in water solution. As depicted in Fig. S10, the calcined uCDs@SiO2 were immersed in water. Visual observation revealed that they remained undissolved, with no significant alteration in particle size compared to their state prior to calcination. To achieve the effective dispersion of uCDs@SiO2 in water, the calcined CDs were ground into fine particles. Subsequently, their various optical properties were characterized.

As shown in Fig. 4 a and Video S2, under 365 nm excitation, uCDs@SiO2 exhibits naked-eye emission in water solution. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, the afterglow emission peak of calcined uCDs@SiO2 in water solution is centered at 580 nm, with a lifetime of 73.63 ms. Further tests were conducted to evaluate its brightness and PL QY, yielding values were 46.62 cd/m2 and 17.15 % respectively (Fig. S11 and Fig. S12). The results indicated that its performance was comparable to that of the uCDs@SiO2 powder, conforming the effectiveness of our adopted strategy in providing robust protection. Additionally, the stability of the uCDs@SiO2 in water solution was evaluated, revealing that even after 8 h, its afterglow emission intensity retained approximately 76 % of the initial level (Fig. 4d). Although the high-temperature calcination process can convert the loosely structured uCDs@SiO2 into a solid and dense silicon-oxygen network, it still fails to achieve complete isolation of CDs from water. As the reaction time prolongs, the afterglow performance will gradually decay, which is consistent with the results reported in previous research [39].

As observed in the thermogravimetric (TG) (Fig. S13), the weight of CDs showed minimal decrease between 100 and 200 °C, suggesting that the changes in the spatial constrain structure are primarily due to variations in the calcination temperature. According to previous literature, the high-temperature calcination process not only transforms the loosely structured uCDs@SiO2 into a robust and tight silica network but also diminishes oxygen-related surface defects on the CDs [18,39]. On the one hand, this rigid network effectively prevents direct contact between CDs and water [40]. On the other hand, the substantial reduction in surface defects significantly suppresses non-radiative transition processes. Based on these mechanisms, CDs are able to sustain high-efficiency afterglow emission in aqueous environments [41,42].

Utilizing the afterglow luminous properties, a time-delay LED has been developed that continues to emit afterglow even after the power is turned off. This feature provides sufficient time for human eyes to adjust, thereby mitigating the temporary blindness effect caused by sudden darkness. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, when a 365 nm LED chip is used as the excitation source, uCDs@SiO2 exhibits visible afterglow emission after the power is switched off. Owing to its straightforward synthesis and low cost, uCDs@SiO2 demonstrates significant potential in the field of time-delayed lighting.

In consideration of the exceptional afterglow properties and remarkable stability in water solution, uCDs@SiO2 can be effectively utilized for information encryption. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, we constructed the numerical sequence “888″ utilizing yellow phosphor, uCDs@SiO2, and calcined uCDs@SiO2. The yellow segments were filled with yellow phosphor, the red segments with uCDs@SiO2, and the green segments with calcined uCDs@SiO2. Under UV irradiation, the incorrect message “888” was observed. Upon turned off the excitation, the yellow phosphor, which lacks afterglow emission, revealed an error message “767". Only when the number “888″ was immersed in water for 2 h, causing the quenching of uCDs@SiO2, did the correct information “151″ become visible after the excitation was turned off. Comparison to conventional information encryption, this approach offers enhanced privacy and exhibits significant potential for applications requiring advanced levels of information encryption.

In summary, uCDs were initially synthesized through the reaction between seed CDs and urea. These uCDs exhibited weak afterglow emission attributed to hydrogen bonding. Subsequently, by encapsulating uCDs within a SiO2 matrix (uCDs@SiO2), the afterglow emission was significantly enhanced. This enhancement is mainly due to the formation of numerous stable covalent bonds during the addition of APTES, which effectively stabilized triplet excitons and suppressed non-radiative transitions. The resulting uCDs@SiO2 powder not only exhibits a prolonged duration of visible afterglow emission under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm but also demonstrates improved PL QY. Furthermore, by calcining uCDs@SiO2, afterglow emission can also be achieved in water solution. This is because the high-temperature calcination process transforms the loosely uCDs@SiO2 into a robust and compact silica network while simultaneously establishing stable covalent interactions between CDs and silica network under multiple constraints. Finally, leveraging its unique optical properties, uCDs@SiO2 holds potential for applications in LED devices and advanced information encryption.

Yulong Zhu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Chao Li: Writing – review & editing. Jinhao Zang: Supervision, Investigation. Guangsong Zheng: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yang Nan: Writing – review & editing. Xiaofan Xia: Data curation. Zhichao Zhu: Data curation. Guoqin Cao: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Junhua Hu: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Jinyang Zhu: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of Zhongyuan Critical Metals Laboratory (GJJSGFYQ202401), Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2024M762975), Henan Province Science and Technology Research and Development Project 202102210220. This work was also supported by Frontier Exploration Project of Longmen Laboratory (LMOYTSKT009).
